part: A Richmond Childhood Behind the Threats
title: It's Your Interview, You're in Charge
summary: David Cline of Virginia Tech and the Southern Oral History Program opens the Civil Rights History Project session in Richmond, Virginia, on August 17, 2013, with John Bishop behind the camera, explaining his chronological family-history approach before introducing Oliver W. Hill Jr.

part: A Richmond Childhood Behind the Threats
title: A Circumscribed World
summary: Hill recounts being born in 1949 in Richmond's Black hospital, now a Virginia Union University dorm, and the self-contained, class-heterogeneous Black neighborhood where a lawyer lived beside a plumber and one rarely needed to leave, the hub of his first dozen years.

part: A Richmond Childhood Behind the Threats
title: Somebody Else's Pathology
summary: Hill describes his father's NAACP Legal Defense Fund work, the telephone threats that kept him from answering the phone and a cross burning when he was six or seven, his parents' effort to teach him that prejudice was others' pathology, and the white-normed media that still shaped a boy who wanted wavy hair like Elvis Presley.

part: His Father's Road to the Law
title: Raised by the Pentecosts
summary: Hill traces his father Oliver Hill Sr., born in Richmond in 1907, deserted by his own father, raised partly by a stern, well-read foster family named Pentecost in Roanoke while his mother took seasonal hotel work, and sent to Washington's prestigious Dunbar High School because Roanoke had no Black high school.

part: His Father's Road to the Law
title: Social Engineer or Social Parasite
topic: Howard Law and the Plessy Strategy
summary: Hill tells how an uncle's annotated constitution made his father question segregation's legality and drew him to Howard Law, remade by President Mordecai Wyatt Johnson and dean Charles Hamilton Houston, where classmate Thurgood Marshall and Houston's charge to be social engineers launched the strategy of attacking Plessy v. Ferguson.

part: His Father's Road to the Law
title: Waiting Tables in the Depression
topic: Building the LDF and the War
summary: Hill recounts his father graduating in 1933, waiting tables in Depression-era Roanoke, joining the close Howard-graduate circle of NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund lawyers, winning the Virginia teacher salary case, and being drafted in his forties for World War II while repeatedly demanding officer training.

part: His Father's Road to the Law
title: Knocking on White Doors
summary: Hill describes his father serving in an all-Black unit in England and France, then returning to become the first Black person elected to office in the South since Reconstruction on the Richmond City Council, canvassing white neighborhoods, losing a House of Delegates race by about forty votes, and attending every political meeting in town.

part: Brown and the Virginia Cases
title: Tar Paper Shacks in Farmville
summary: Hill recounts the call from Barbara Rose Johns, a high school girl who organized a walkout over Farmville's tar-paper-shack conditions, and how her persistence drew his father to a parents' meeting where he warned the case would now attack segregation per se, making Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County part of the Brown package.

part: Brown and the Virginia Cases
title: Ten Years Before Rosa Parks
summary: Hill notes that the Davis case took from 1952 to the 1954 Brown decision, that Barbara Rose Johns suffered and left Farmville, that her uncle was the preacher Vernon Johns who preceded Martin Luther King in Alabama, and that a decade before Rosa Parks his father's firm won the Irene Morgan case ending interstate transportation segregation.

part: Brown and the Virginia Cases
title: Strategy Sessions at the Breakfast Table
summary: Hill describes the lawyers' incremental two-decade strategy, traveling attorneys like Thurgood Marshall and Robert L. Carter lodging at the Hill home because hotels were closed to them, his father's partnership with Spottswood W. Robinson III, and Virginia's massive resistance under the Byrd Machine and Governor J. Lindsay Almond Jr.

part: One of the First to Desegregate
title: Desegregation but Not Integration
summary: Hill explains the post-Brown decade of compliance cases, his own place among the first Virginia students to desegregate schools around 1961, the pupil placement board that assigned schools, his father's good relationship with progressive school-board head Lewis Powell, and his father's distinction that a token few Black students made a school desegregated but not integrated.

part: One of the First to Desegregate
title: Walking Up the Empty Steps
summary: Hill recalls not wanting to leave his Black-school friends, officials measuring distances with yardsticks to exclude a friend three feet too far from Chandler Middle School, the surreal first day when his father's partner Henry L. Marsh III drove him to a deserted-looking white school where Black students were marched two by two to homerooms, and the football team that, over the years, turned even prejudiced classmates into teammates as a Black prom queen and easing social segregation followed.

part: White Flight and the Color of Class
title: A Four-to-Four Split
summary: Hill recounts busing as a remedy and Judge Robert R. Merhige Jr ordering cross-county busing despite threats and his dog being killed, then the Supreme Court's four-to-four split, after Lewis F. Powell Jr recused himself over his Richmond school ties while William Rehnquist did not, upholding the lower court and killing the county-inclusive busing plan.

part: White Flight and the Color of Class
title: Race Morphed Into Class
summary: Hill compares Louisville merging city and county and describes Richmond's white power structure annexing county land until the Voting Rights Act blocked it as vote dilution, then an annexation moratorium leaving the city land-locked, and the old racial politics of Virginia's independent cities now clothed as class conflict.

part: Howard and the Inward Turn
title: Tarzan Movies to African Civilizations
summary: A little miffed but persuaded of the cause, Hill describes walking to Chandler Middle School from Northside, then Howard University in the late sixties, an eye-opening African history class after a childhood that knew only Tarzan movies, and campus black power figures Stokely Carmichael and H. Rap Brown challenging King's nonviolence with black nationalism.

part: Howard and the Inward Turn
title: An Inward Turn to Indian Philosophy
summary: Hill recounts black nationalism holding sway a year or two before an inward turn to Eastern philosophy, a senior perception-psychology course on how belief constructs reality, becoming a meditation teacher in Boston and Detroit en route to the University of Michigan, and framing nonviolence and dignified non-reaction as a practice of mindfulness rooted in Gandhi and ahimsa.

part: What Integration Cost the Classroom
title: The Best and Brightest Left Teaching
summary: Hill argues integration cost Black schools their gifted women teachers as the women's movement opened other careers, displaced Black principals, and ended the mission-driven, class-mixed schools of the segregation era, leaving inner-city hubs of poverty, and points to Petersburg's lowest-performing schools, eroding tax base, and revolving superintendents as the result.

part: Monuments and a Father's Recognition
title: Slavery as a Benign Institution
summary: Hill recalls Virginia history class teaching slavery as a benign, family-like institution he knew was false, and a succession of governors beginning with L. Douglas Wilder adding the Black story to the record, acknowledging that more civil rights cases arose in Virginia than any other Southern state in a climate that, unlike Mississippi, let people feel safer standing up.

part: Monuments and a Father's Recognition
title: Arthur Ashe on Monument Avenue
topic: Confederate Statues and a New Memorial
summary: Hill describes Monument Avenue's towering Confederate generals facing north or south by their war fate, the uproar over adding an Arthur Ashe statue, an earlier objection to a Lincoln statue at the Tredegar Iron Works, and the Capitol-grounds Virginia civil rights memorial picturing Barbara Rose Johns, his father, and Robinson near a statue of Harry F. Byrd Sr.

part: Monuments and a Father's Recognition
title: His Name on the Old Finance Building
summary: Hill notes the irony of his father's name on the Capitol's old finance building, where Byrd's circle plotted massive resistance and where his father once had to fight segregated seating to take the bar exam, and recounts the national recognition his father received in his last decade, including the 1999 Presidential Medal of Freedom, before the interview turns to Hill's own career.

part: A Psychologist's Fight for the Mind
title: Orange and Blue Hand-Me-Downs
summary: Hill describes his career at Virginia State University, an HBCU whose orange-and-blue colors came from UVA football hand-me-downs, the politics of state funding, and his research on Black education and cognitive factors, including how early childhood stimulation and the once-fixed-now-malleable view of cognitive ability shape achievement.

part: A Psychologist's Fight for the Mind
title: A Civil Rights Approach to Math
topic: Bob Moses and the Algebra Project
summary: Hill recounts partnering with Bob Moses and the Algebra Project in Petersburg, Moses being a sixties Mississippi voter-registration organizer who earned a Harvard math PhD and taught abstract math through children's life experiences while applying civil rights community-organizing to school reform, and an interview with Los Angeles educator Mildred Pitts Walter who wrote her own Black children's books.

part: A Psychologist's Fight for the Mind
title: A Framework for the Content
summary: Hill explains looking for transfer from cognitive-skill practice like attention and short-term memory to math performance, likening cognitive training to a framework that content must adhere to, laments a dumbing down of education, and blames well-meaning liberal teachers who held Black students to lower standards, recalling his educator mother's observation, citing George Bush's phrase about low expectations, and arguing that challenging curricula, not remediation, are what works.

part: A Psychologist's Fight for the Mind
title: Everything Is About the Test
summary: Hill calls Virginia's ironically named Standards of Learning a barrier to innovation in districts like Petersburg where everything turns on test scores, describes Virginia Tech workshops where teachers hungry for African American and civil rights history struggle to fit it into the SOLs, and concludes the factory model demands radical restructuring.

part: Still Fighting the Same Battle
title: Where Is the Outrage
summary: Hill voices optimism that an entrenched educational bureaucracy will change slowly, predicts MOOCs and the decline of the university, recalls inspiring teachers who passed on excitement rather than rote test prep, and frames the present fight as the same battle in a new form: racial segregation become class segregation in disgraceful city schools, with the Iraq war's trillion dollars found while education is neglected, naming education the movement's legacy and asking where the outrage is over school conditions and mass incarceration, citing The New Jim Crow before closing.
